content:
Arrange the complex ions in the increasing order of their magnetic moments

A. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$
B. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$
C. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$
D. $\left[\mathrm{FeF}_6\right]^{3-}$
explanation:
Let’s count the unpaired electrons in each complex (spin‐only magnetic moment $$\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)}$$, where $n$=number of unpaired electrons):

[Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻

• Fe²⁺ is d⁶, CN⁻ is strong‐field → low‐spin: t₂g⁶ eg⁰ ⇒ n=0

[Fe(CN)₆]³⁻

• Fe³⁺ is d⁵, CN⁻ is strong‐field → low‐spin: t₂g⁵ eg⁰ ⇒ n=1

[Fe(H₂O)₆]²⁺

• Fe²⁺ is d⁶, H₂O is weak‐field → high‐spin: t₂g⁴ eg² ⇒ n=4

[FeF₆]³⁻

• Fe³⁺ is d⁵, F⁻ is weak‐field → high‐spin: t₂g³ eg² ⇒ n=5

Ordering by increasing $n$:

[Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻ (0) < [Fe(CN)₆]³⁻ (1) < [Fe(H₂O)₆]²⁺ (4) < [FeF₆]³⁻ (5)

That matches Option B.

content: Which of the following compounds has electrons symmetrically distributed in both $\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}$ and $\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}$ orbitals?
explanation:
Let’s work through the d-electron count and spin state for each:

[CoF₆]³⁻

– Co³⁺ is d⁶; F⁻ is a weak‐field ligand ⇒ high‐spin

– High‐spin d⁶:

$$t_{2g}^4\,e_g^2$$

– t₂g has one orbital doubly occupied ⇒ not symmetric

[Mn(CN)₆]⁴⁻

– Mn²⁺ is d⁵; CN⁻ is a strong‐field ligand ⇒ low‐spin

– Low‐spin d⁵:

$$t_{2g}^5\,e_g^0$$

– eg set empty (trivial symmetry) but t₂g is unevenly filled (2,2,1)

[Cr(NH₃)₆]³⁺

– Cr³⁺ is d³; regardless of field strength d³ ⇒

$$t_{2g}^3\,e_g^0$$

– eg empty, only t₂g occupied

[FeCl₆]³⁻

– Fe³⁺ is d⁵; Cl⁻ is weak-field ⇒ high-spin

– High-spin d⁵:

$$t_{2g}^3\,e_g^2$$

– t₂g³ ⇒ one electron in each of the three t₂g orbitals

eg² ⇒ one electron in each of the two e_g orbitals

– both sets are equally (symmetrically) occupied

Answer: Option D, [FeCl₆]³⁻.

content:
Which of the coordination compounds [(i) to (v)] are used for the following processes :

A: Electroplating

B: Removal of excess copper from human body

C: Estimation of hardness of water

(i) DMG (ii) $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{EDTA}$ (iii) Cis-platin (iv) D-penicillamine (v) $\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Au}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]$
explanation:
Here’s how they match up:

Electroplating

• Compound (v): $$K[Au(CN)_2]$$

Removal of excess copper from the body

• Compound (iv): D-penicillamine

Estimation of water hardness

• Compound (ii): $$\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{EDTA}$$

So the correct choice is Option C:

A = v, B = iv, C = ii.

content:
Which one of the following structures in Column I does not have the correct IUPAC name as given in Column II.

S.No. Structure IUPAC name
A. $\left[\mathrm{CoBr}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}$ P. Dibromidodi-( ethan-1, 2 - diamine)cobalt (III) chloride
B. $\mathrm{Na}\left[\mathrm{PtBrCl}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\right]$ Q. Sodium amminebromidochloridonitrito- N - palatinate(II)
C. $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_2\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{SO}_4$ R. Tetraamminediaquachloridochromium (III) sulphate.
D. $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_4\right]$ S. Tetraammineplatinum (II) tetrachloridoplatinate (II)
explanation:
Let’s check each pairing:

A ↔ P

– [CoBr₂(en)₂]Cl is correctly named “dibromido(di­ethane-1,2-diamine)cobalt(III) chloride.”

– P matches.

C ↔ R

– [Cr(H₂O)₂(NH₃)₄Cl]₂⁺·SO₄²⁻ is “tetraammine­diaqua­chloridochromium(III) sulfate.”

– R matches.

D ↔ S

– [Pt(NH₃)₄]²⁺ is “tetraammineplatinum(II)” and [PtCl₄]²⁻ is “tetrachloridoplatinate(II).”

– S matches.

B ↔ Q

– The given name Q (“sodium amminebromidochloridonitrito-N-palatinate(II)”) is flawed:

• It misspells “platinate” as “palatinate.”

• Nitrite bound via N should be “nitro-κN” (or simply “nitro”), not “nitrito-N.”

– Q is incorrect.

Therefore the only wrong match is B.

Answer: B.

content: A transition metal M forms 4 homoleptic octahedral coordination compounds, $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D of the type $\left[\mathrm{MX}_6\right]^{z-}$ with monodentate ligands a, b, c and d respectively. These compounds absorb red. blue. yellow and blue-green light respectively. Which one of the options shows the correct order of decreasing ligand strength?
explanation:
Ligand strength in an octahedral complex is proportional to the crystal‐field splitting Δ, which in turn is the energy of the absorbed photon:

$$Δ = \frac{hc}{λ}$$

– shorter λ ⇒ larger Δ ⇒ stronger ligand.

Compounds absorb:

• A → red (≈700 nm) ⇒ smallest Δ

• C → yellow (≈580 nm)

• D → blue–green (≈490 nm)

• B → blue (≈450 nm) ⇒ largest Δ

So in order of decreasing ligand strength (largest Δ first):

B > D > C > A

That corresponds to Option A.

content: Choose the coordination compound which does not exhibit Optical activity.
explanation:
The only complex in which a mirror‐plane (or inversion center) survives is the trans isomer. In all the others the arrangement of the bidentate en ligands is helical (Δ/Λ) and you get optical isomers.

Answer: Option C,

trans–$\bigl[\mathrm{CoCl}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\bigr]^+$ is achiral and does not show optical activity.

Why trans is achiral:

The two Cl⁻ ligands sit 180° apart, defining an inversion center (or a σ-plane through Co and bisecting opposite N–Co–N angles).

Each en chelate then sits symmetrically on either side of that element, so the mirror image is superimposable.

content:
The correct order of spin only magnetic moments among the following is:
[Given: Atomic numbers: $\mathrm{Mn}=25, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Co}=27$ ]
explanation:
Let’s work out the number of unpaired electrons (n) and the spin-only moment

$$\mu_{\rm spin}=\sqrt{n(n+2)}\;\mathrm{BM}$$

for each complex:

[Fe(CN)₆]⁴⁻

• Fe oxidation state: +2 (CN⁻×6 gives –6; Fe–6=–4 ⇒ Fe=+2)

• d⁶, CN⁻ is a strong‐field ligand ⇒ low‐spin t₂g⁶ → n=0

• μ = √[0·(0+2)] = 0 BM

[MnCl₄]²⁻

• Mn oxidation state: +2 (Cl⁻×4 gives –4; Mn–4=–2 ⇒ Mn=+2)

• d⁵, Cl⁻ is weak‐field ⇒ high‐spin → n=5

• μ = √[5·(5+2)] = √35 ≈ 5.92 BM

[CoCl₄]²⁻

• Co oxidation state: +2 (same reasoning)

• d⁷, weak‐field tetrahedral ⇒ high‐spin → n=3

• μ = √[3·(3+2)] = √15 ≈ 3.87 BM

Ranking from largest to smallest μ gives:

$$[\mathrm{MnCl}_4]^{2-}>\;[\mathrm{CoCl}_4]^{2-}>\;[\mathrm{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$$

which is Option B.

content: An aqueous solution of $\mathrm{CrCl}_3 .6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ (Molar mass $=266.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) containing 2.665 g of the solute after processing, when treated with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ gave 2.87 g of AgCl (Molar mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}=143.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) Choose the correct formula of the compound which give these results.
explanation:
First, find how many moles of complex and Cl⁻ were present:

Moles of “CrCl₃·6H₂O” sample

$$n_{\rm complex}=\frac{2.665\ {\rm g}}{266.5\ {\rm g/mol}}=0.010\ {\rm mol}$$

Moles of AgCl precipitated (each AgCl consumes one Cl⁻)

$$n_{\rm AgCl}=\frac{2.87\ {\rm g}}{143.5\ {\rm g/mol}}=0.020\ {\rm mol}$$

Free Cl⁻ per mole of complex

$$\frac{0.020}{0.010}=2\ {\rm Cl}^-$$

Only the formula with two ionic chlorides is

$$[\mathrm{Cr}(H_2O)_5Cl]Cl_2\cdot H_2O$$

which is Option C.

content: Which one of the following coordination compounds will exhibit both geometrical and optical isomerism?
explanation:
The correct choice is option B:

$$\bigl[\mathrm{Co}(\text{en})_2\mathrm{Cl}_2\bigr]^+$$

Here’s why:

A: $[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{ox})_3]^{3-}$

Three identical bidentate oxalate ligands.

Only one geometrical arrangement (all three ligands equivalent).

It does give optical isomers (Δ/Λ), but no geometrical isomers.

B: $[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_2\mathrm{Cl}_2]^+$

Two bidentate en (ethylenediamine) and two monodentate Cl⁻.

Geometrical isomers: cis and trans (Cl’s can be adjacent or opposite).

The cis form is chiral (Δ/Λ enantiomers), so it also shows optical isomerism.

C: $[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6]^{3-}$

Six identical monodentate CN⁻ ligands.

Only one octahedral form—no geometric or optical isomers.

D: $[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{NO}_3)_3(\mathrm{NH}_3)_3]$

Three NO₃⁻ and three NH₃ (monodentate each).

You get fac and mer geometrical isomers, but neither is chiral (both are superimposable on their mirror images).

Therefore, only B exhibits both geometrical (cis/trans) and optical (enantiomeric) isomerism.

content:
Arrange the following compounds in the decreasing order of the molar conductivities of their aqueous solutions.

A B C D
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$ $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{Cl}_3\right]$ $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}$ $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3$
explanation:
The key is that molar conductivity scales with the total number of ions produced on dissolving:

D = $\bigl[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_6}\bigr]\mathrm{Cl}_3$ → $\mathrm{[Co(NH_3)_6]^{3+}}$ + 3 Cl⁻ ⇒ 4 ions

A = $\bigl[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_5Cl}\bigr]\mathrm{Cl}_2$ → $\mathrm{[Co(NH_3)_5Cl]^{2+}}$ + 2 Cl⁻ ⇒ 3 ions

C = $\bigl[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_4Cl_2}\bigr]\mathrm{Cl}$ → $\mathrm{[Co(NH_3)_4Cl_2]^{+}}$ + Cl⁻ ⇒ 2 ions

B = $\bigl[\mathrm{Co(NH_3)_3Cl_3}\bigr]$ is neutral ⇒ 0 ions

Ordering these from most to fewest ions gives:

$$D>A>C>B$$

Hence the correct choice is Option B.

content:
Match the coordination compounds in Column I having the given type of hybridisation of $\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+}$ ion and magnetic moment as given in Column II.

Coordination compounds Hybridisation & Magnetic nature
A. $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ P. $\mathrm{sp}^3, \quad \mu=5.92 \mathrm{BM}$
B. $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ Q. $\mathrm{sp}^3, \quad \mu=2.84 \mathrm{BM}$
C. $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{Cl})_4\right]^{2-}$ R. $\mathrm{sp}^3, \quad \mu=0$
D. $\left[\mathrm{MnBr}_4\right]^{2-}$ S. $\mathrm{dsp}^2, \quad \mu=0$
explanation:
Here’s the match-up with a brief rationale for each:

Ni(CO)₄

Ni^0 is 3d¹⁰; CO is a strong‐field ligand → all electrons paired

Tetrahedral uses sp³ → μ = 0

→ R

[Ni(CN)₄]²⁻

Ni²⁺ is d⁸; CN⁻ is strong‐field → square-planar, all paired

Hybridization dsp² → μ = 0

→ S

[NiCl₄]²⁻

Ni²⁺ is d⁸; Cl⁻ is weak‐field → tetrahedral, two unpaired

Hybridization sp³ →

$$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)} = \sqrt{2\cdot4} \approx 2.84\;\mathrm{BM}$$

→ Q

[MnBr₄]²⁻

Mn²⁺ is d⁵; Br⁻ is weak‐field → tetrahedral, five unpaired

Hybridization sp³ →

$$\mu = \sqrt{5(5+2)} = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.92\;\mathrm{BM}$$

→ P

Thus: A = R, B = S, C = Q, D = P, which is Option A.

content:
Which of the following 2 compounds exhibit both Geometrical and Structural isomerism?

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right] \mathrm{NO}_2 \\ & \mathrm{~B}=\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right) \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_4 \\ & \mathrm{C}=\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_3\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_3\right] \\ & \mathrm{D}=\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3 \end{aligned}$$

Based on Valence Bond Theory, match the complexes listed in Column I with the number of unpaired electrons on the central metal ion, given in Column II

No. Complex ions No. Number of unpaired electrons
(A) $$
\left[\mathrm{Fe} \mathrm{F}_6\right]^{3-}
$$		 (P) 0
(B) $$
\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}
$$		 (P) 1
(C) $$
\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}
$$		 (R) 5
(D) $$
\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}
$$		 (S) 4

Given below are 4 statements. Two of these are correct statements. Identify them.

A. $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$ is easily oxidised to $$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$$ in the presence of a strong ligand like $$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$

B. $$[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6]^{4-}$$ is an octahedral complex ion which is paramagnetic in nature.

C. Removal of $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ molecules from $$[\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O})_6] \mathrm{Cl}_3$$ on strong heating converts it to a colourless compound.

D. Crystal Field splitting in Octahedral and Tetrahedral complexes is given by the equation $$\Delta_0=4 / 9 \Delta_t$$
explanation:
The correct answer is Option A: A & C.

Let's analyze each statement:

A. $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$ is easily oxidised to $$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$$ in the presence of a strong ligand like $$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$

This statement is correct. Here's why:

  • Cobalt(II) ($$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$) has a d7 electronic configuration, making it relatively stable. However, the presence of a strong ligand like cyanide ($$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$) can significantly alter its stability.
  • Strong field ligands like cyanide cause a large crystal field splitting, leading to a low-spin configuration for $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$. This low-spin configuration has all the electrons paired up in the lower energy d orbitals.
  • The pairing of electrons in the d orbitals makes the oxidation of $$\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$$ to $$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$$ (d6 configuration) more favorable due to the increased stability of the low-spin d6 configuration in the presence of the strong field ligand.

B. $$[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6]^{4-}$$ is an octahedral complex ion which is paramagnetic in nature.

This statement is incorrect. Here's why:

  • The complex ion $$[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6]^{4-}$$ is indeed octahedral, but it is diamagnetic, not paramagnetic.
  • Iron(II) ($$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$$) has a d6 electronic configuration. Cyanide ($$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$$), being a strong field ligand, causes a large crystal field splitting, resulting in a low-spin configuration.
  • In the low-spin configuration, all six electrons in the d orbitals are paired, making the complex diamagnetic (no unpaired electrons).

C. Removal of $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ molecules from $$[\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O})_6] \mathrm{Cl}_3$$ on strong heating converts it to a colourless compound.

This statement is correct. Here's why:

  • The complex $$[\mathrm{Ti}(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O})_6] \mathrm{Cl}_3$$ is colored because the d orbitals of titanium(III) ($$\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$$) are split by the ligand field, allowing for d-d electronic transitions that absorb certain wavelengths of light, resulting in a color.
  • Strong heating removes the water molecules, leading to the formation of anhydrous titanium(III) chloride ($$\mathrm{TiCl}_3$$).
  • Anhydrous titanium(III) chloride is colorless because in the absence of water ligands, the d orbitals are no longer split, and d-d transitions are not possible, meaning no light is absorbed.

D. Crystal Field splitting in Octahedral and Tetrahedral complexes is given by the equation $$\Delta_0=4 / 9 \Delta_t$$

This statement is incorrect. Here's why:

  • The correct relationship between the crystal field splitting in octahedral ($$\Delta_0$$) and tetrahedral ($$\Delta_t$$) complexes is: $$\Delta_t = (4/9)\Delta_0$$.
  • This means that the crystal field splitting in tetrahedral complexes is approximately four-ninths of the splitting in octahedral complexes.
  • The reason for this difference is the geometric arrangement of the ligands. In an octahedral complex, the ligands are arranged at 90 degrees to each other, resulting in a stronger interaction with the metal d orbitals. In a tetrahedral complex, the ligands are at 109.5 degrees to each other, leading to a weaker interaction with the d orbitals.

In conclusion, the correct statements are A and C, explaining the oxidation behavior of cobalt in the presence of strong ligands and the color change associated with the dehydration of titanium(III) complexes.

content:
Based on Crystal Field theory, match the Complex ions listed in Column I with the electronic configuration in the d orbitals of the central metal ion listed in Column II.

No. Complexion No. d orbital configuration of central metal ion.
(A) $$
\left[M n(C N)_6\right]^{4-}
$$		 (P) $$
e_g^2 t_{2 g}^3
$$
(B) $$
\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}
$$		 (Q) $$
t_{2 g}^4 e_g^2
$$
(C) $$
\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}
$$		 (R) $$
t_{2 g}^5
$$
(D) $$
\left[\mathrm{MnCl}_4\right]^{2-}
$$		 (S) $$
t_{2 g}^5 e_g^2
$$
explanation:
Let's analyze each complex ion to determine the correct electronic configuration based on Crystal Field Theory:

(A) $$[Mn(CN)_6]^{4-}$$

  • Mn in +2 oxidation state, so its electronic configuration is $3d^5$.

  • CN- is a strong field ligand, causing a large splitting of the d orbitals.

  • Therefore, all five electrons will occupy the lower energy $$t_{2g}$$ orbitals, leading to the configuration $$t_{2g}^5$$, which corresponds to (R).

(B) $$[Co(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$$

  • Co in +2 oxidation state, its electronic configuration is $3d^7$.

  • H2O is a weak field ligand, resulting in a smaller splitting of the d orbitals.

  • Therefore, the electronic configuration will be $$t_{2g}^5 e_g^2$$, which corresponds to (S).

(C) $$[Fe(H_2O)_6]^{2+}$$

  • Fe in +2 oxidation state, its electronic configuration is $3d^6$.

  • H2O is a weak field ligand, leading to a smaller splitting of the d orbitals.

  • Therefore, the electronic configuration will be $$t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$$, which corresponds to (Q).

(D) $$[MnCl_4]^{2-}$$

  • Mn in +2 oxidation state, its electronic configuration is $3d^5$.

  • Cl- is a weak field ligand. The complex is tetrahedral, which results in a different splitting pattern than octahedral complexes.

  • In a tetrahedral complex, the $$e_g$$ orbitals are lower in energy than the $$t_{2g}$$ orbitals.

  • Therefore, the electronic configuration will be $$e_g^2 t_{2g}^3$$, which corresponds to (P).

Therefore, the correct matching is:

A = R

B = S

C = Q

D = P

So the final answer is Option B.

content: On the basis of crystal field theory, electronic configuration of a low spin $$\mathrm{d}^4$$ complex is:
explanation:
For a low spin $$\mathrm{d}^4$$ octahedral complex, the strong crystal field (i.e. large splitting energy, $$\Delta_o$$) means that electrons prefer to pair up in the lower energy $$t_{2g}$$ orbitals rather than occupy the higher energy $$e_g$$ orbitals. Here's the reasoning:

In an octahedral field, the five d orbitals split into:

Three lower energy orbitals: $$t_{2g}$$

Two higher energy orbitals: $$e_g$$

For a low spin configuration, the splitting energy $$\Delta_o$$ is larger than the pairing energy. Thus, electrons minimize energy by pairing in the lower $$t_{2g}$$ orbitals.

For a $$\mathrm{d}^4$$ system:

The first three electrons each occupy one of the three $$t_{2g}$$ orbitals.

The fourth electron, even though it must pair up with one of the electrons (incurring a pairing energy cost), will still go into a $$t_{2g}$$ orbital because putting it into an $$e_g$$ orbital would require lifting it to a much higher energy level.

The resulting electron configuration is:

$$ t_{2g}^4 \; e_g^0 $$

Looking at the provided options, Option B (written as $$t_{2g}^4 e_g$$) corresponds to the configuration $$t_{2g}^4$$ with no electrons in $$e_g$$ (implicitly, $$e_g^0$$).

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B.

content: The molar conductivity of the complex $$\mathrm{CoCl}_3 \cdot 4 \mathrm{NH}_3 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ is found to be the same as that of a $$1: 3$$ electrolyte. The structural formula of the compound is :
explanation:
To determine the correct structure, consider the information regarding the electrolyte behavior. A 1:3 electrolyte means that when the compound dissolves, it dissociates into one cation and three anions. For the given complex, this implies that the coordination sphere must contain all the neutral ligands, while all chloride ions forming the counterions are outside the coordination sphere.

Let's analyze the options:

Option A:

$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)_2\right]\mathrm{Cl}_3$$

Here, cobalt is coordinated by 4 ammonia molecules and 2 water molecules, forming an octahedral complex.

All 3 chloride ions are outside the square brackets.

When this complex dissolves, it yields one complex cation:

$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)_2\right]^{3+}$$

along with three free $$\mathrm{Cl}^-$$ ions.

This exactly matches the behavior of a 1:3 electrolyte.

Option B:

$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\mathrm{Cl}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)\right]\mathrm{Cl}_2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$

In this case, one chloride is coordinated, so the complex ion has a lower positive charge and would yield only two free chloride ions upon dissolution, making it a 1:2 electrolyte.

Option C:

$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]\mathrm{Cl}\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$

Here, two chlorides are inside the coordination sphere, resulting in a complex with only one free chloride ion (1:1 electrolyte).

Option D:

$$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)_2\mathrm{Cl}\right]\mathrm{Cl}_2$$

In this option, one chloride is coordinated and two are free, leading to a 1:2 electrolyte.

Since only Option A dissociates to yield one complex cation and three free chloride ions, it is the correct structure.

Answer:

$$\boxed{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)_2\right]\mathrm{Cl}_3}$$

content: The type of hybridisation and the spin only magnetic moment for the complex $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4]$$ are respectively:
explanation:
Let's analyze the complex $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4]$$ step by step:

$$\textbf{Oxidation State and Electron Configuration:}$$

Carbon monoxide (CO) is a neutral ligand.

Therefore, the oxidation state of nickel in this complex is 0.

Elemental nickel has the electron configuration $$\mathrm{[Ar]\,3d^8\,4s^2}$$.

In the case of $$\mathrm{Ni^0}$$ complexes like $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4]$$, the electrons rearrange such that all the $$d$$ orbitals become fully occupied (i.e., a $$d^{10}$$ configuration).

With a $$d^{10}$$ configuration, there are no unpaired electrons.

$$\textbf{Magnetic Moment:}$$

The spin-only magnetic moment is calculated using the formula:

$$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}\ \text{BM}$$

where $$n$$ is the number of unpaired electrons.

Since $$n = 0$$ here, the magnetic moment is:

$$\mu = \sqrt{0(0+2)} = 0\ \text{BM}$$

This confirms that the complex is diamagnetic (zero magnetic moment).

$$\textbf{Hybridisation and Geometry:}$$

$$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4]$$ is a tetrahedral complex.

In tetrahedral complexes with a coordination number of 4, the central metal atom typically adopts $$\mathrm{sp^3}$$ hybridisation.

Putting these points together:

The hybridisation is $$\mathrm{sp^3}$$.

The spin-only magnetic moment is zero.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C: $$\mathrm{sp^3}$$, zero.

content: A d - block metal $$\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{Z}=26)$$ forms a compound $$[\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{CN})_2(\mathrm{CO})_4]^{+}$$. Calculate its spin magnetic moment value.
explanation:
To calculate the spin magnetic moment of the compound $$[\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{CN})_2(\mathrm{CO})_4]^{+}$$, we need to follow these steps:

1. Determine the oxidation state of the metal $$\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{Z}=26)$$.

The metal $$\mathrm{X}$$ is iron (Fe) since it has an atomic number of 26. Let the oxidation state of Fe in the complex be $$\mathrm{x}$$.

Given the ligands, CN (cyanide) and CO (carbon monoxide), both are considered to be strong field ligands which typically cause pairing of electrons.

The overall charge of the complex is +1:

$$\mathrm{x} + 2(-1) + 4(0) = +1$$

$$\mathrm{x} - 2 = +1$$

$$\mathrm{x} = +3$$

So, the oxidation state of Fe in the compound is +3.

2. Find the electronic configuration of $$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$$:

The electronic configuration of neutral Fe is:

$$ \mathrm{[Ar] 3d^6 4s^2} $$

When Fe loses 3 electrons to form $$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$$, the configuration becomes:

$$ \mathrm{[Ar] 3d^5} $$

3. Determine the number of unpaired electrons:

Since $$\mathrm{CN^-}$$ and $$\mathrm{CO}$$ are strong field ligands, they will pair up the electrons in the 3d orbitals:

$$ \text{3d orbitals with strong field ligands:} $$

$$ \uparrow\downarrow\uparrow\downarrow\uparrow\downarrow\uparrow\uparrow $$

This configuration shows that there is only one unpaired electron in $$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$$.

4. Calculate the spin magnetic moment:

The spin-only formula for the magnetic moment $$\mu_{\text{spin}}$$ is given by:

$$ \mu_{\text{spin}} = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \, \text{BM} $$

Where $$n$$ is the number of unpaired electrons.

For $$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}$$ in this compound, $$n = 1$$.

$$ \mu_{\text{spin}} = \sqrt{1(1+2)} \, \text{BM} $$

$$ \mu_{\text{spin}} = \sqrt{3} \, \text{BM} $$

$$ \mu_{\text{spin}} \approx 1.73 \, \text{BM} $$

Therefore, the spin magnetic moment value for the compound $$[\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{CN})_2(\mathrm{CO})_4]^{+}$$ is 1.73 BM.

Option B: 1.73 BM

content: A Coordination compound is represented by the formula $$[\mathrm{CoBr}_3(e n) x]$$. This compound required one mole of $$\mathrm{AgNO}_3$$ to form a pale yellow precipitate of $$\mathrm{AgBr}$$. What is the value of $$\mathrm{x}$$ in the compound?
Choose the incorrect statement from the following.

options.content: A tetrahedral complex of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_2 \mathrm{b}_2]$$ does not show geometrical isomerism
Coordination entities of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_3 \mathrm{b}_3]$$ do not exhibit geometrical isomerism
Square planar complex  of the type [Mabcd] has 3 possible geometrical isomers
In a coordination entity of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma} 2(\mathrm{bb})_2]^{2+}$$ only the cis-isomer is optically active

explanation:
Let's analyze each of the given options to determine which one is incorrect:

Option A: A tetrahedral complex of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_2 \mathrm{b}_2]$$ does not show geometrical isomerism.

A tetrahedral complex $$[\mathrm{Ma}_2 \mathrm{b}_2]$$ cannot show geometrical isomerism because the positions of the ligands in a tetrahedral geometry are equivalent. Hence, this statement is correct.

Option B: Coordination entities of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_3 \mathrm{b}_3]$$ do not exhibit geometrical isomerism.

Coordination entities of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_3 \mathrm{b}_3]$$ have a single type of ligand (a and b) in a 3:3 ratio, and the distribution of these ligands can lead to different arrangements. These complexes can exhibit geometrical isomerism. Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

Option C: Square planar complex of the type [Mabcd] has 3 possible geometrical isomers.

In a square planar complex of the type [Mabcd], where M is the metal and a, b, c, d are different ligands, we can indeed have exactly 3 geometrical isomers. Hence, this statement is correct.

Option D: In a coordination entity of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_2(\mathrm{bb})_2]^{2+}$$ only the cis-isomer is optically active.

For a coordination entity of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_2(\mathrm{bb})_2]^{2+}$$, where bb is a bidentate ligand, only the cis-isomer can be optically active as it allows for non-superimposable mirror images. Therefore, this statement is correct.

Based on the analysis, the incorrect statement is:

Option B Coordination entities of the type $$[\mathrm{Ma}_3 \mathrm{b}_3]$$ do not exhibit geometrical isomerism.

content: Which among the following is diamagnetic?
explanation:
In $$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-} \mathrm{CN}^{-}$$ is strong field ligand and causes pairing of electrons.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=3 d^8 \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}} \end{aligned}$$

As, all the electrons are paired, hence, it is diamagnetic.

content: The complex $$\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right]^{2+}$$ ion shows
explanation: $$\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_2(\mathrm{en})_2\right]^{2+}$$ shows both geometrical and optical isomerism as the complex forms cis and trans isomers. trans-isomer doesn't show optical isomerism since.it is symmetrical but cis-isomer shows optical isomerism as it is unsymmetrical.

content: The blue colouration obtained from the Lassaigne's test of nitrogen is due to the formation of
explanation:
The blue colouration is due to the formation of Fe$$_4$$[Fe(CN)$$_6$$]$$_3$$ (ferriferrocyanide).

$$\mathrm{Fe{(OH)_2} + 6NaCN\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {N{a_4}[Fe{{(CN)}_6}]}\limits_{Sodium\,ferrocyanide} + 2NaOH}$$

$$\mathrm{3N{a_4}[Fe{(CN)_6}] + 2F{e_2}{(S{O_4})_3}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {F{e_4}{{[Fe{{(CN)}_6}]}_3}}\limits_{\matrix{ {Ferric\,ferrocyanide} \cr {(\Pr ussian\,blue)} \cr } } + 6N{a_2}S{O_4}}$$

content: Aqueous $$\mathrm{CuSO}_4$$ changes its colour from sky blue to deep blue on addition of $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ because

options.content: $$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4\right]^{2+}$$ is labile complex and changes to $$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}$$ as $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ is stronger ligand than $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

explanation: As, $$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ is a weak field ligand and thus $$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4\right]^{2+}$$ absorbs red light in visible spectrum. But the colour appears blue because $$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4\right]^{2+}$$ is a labile complex and changes to $$\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}$$, where $$\mathrm{NH}_3$$ is strong field ligand thus absorbs yellow light of visible spectrum and impart blue colour.

content:
Which of the following complex show optical isomerism?

(i) $$c i s-\left[\mathrm{COCl}(\mathrm{en})_2\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\right]^{2+}$$

(ii) $$cis-\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_2(\mathrm{ox})_2\right]^{3-}$$

(iii) $$cis-[ \left.\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{en})_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}$$

(iv) $$cis- \left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$$
explanation:
Octahedral complex having general formula $$\left[M(A A)_2 a_2\right]^{n \pm}$$ or $$\left[M(A A)_2 a b\right]^{n \pm}$$, shows optical isomersim.

$$cis- \left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$$ does not show optical isomerism due to symmetry, while other three complexes show optical isomerism.

content: Mohr's salt has the formula
explanation: When $$\mathrm{FeSO}_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ reacts with $$\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$$, it forms a double salt known as ferrous ammonium sulphate $$\left(\mathrm{FeSO}_4\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)$$ or Mohr's salt.

content: Identify the correct IUPAC name of $$[\mathrm{CoCl}_2(\mathrm{NO}_2)(\mathrm{NH}_3)_3]$$
explanation:
The correct IUPAC name for the complex $$[\mathrm{CoCl}_2(\mathrm{NO}_2)(\mathrm{NH}_3)_3]$$ can be determined by following the rules for naming coordination compounds:

  1. Ligands are named first in alphabetical order before the metal ion is named. Here, ammine (old spelling of amine recommended by IUPAC for naming complexes) comes before chloro and nitrito. Remember, nitrito can bind through the nitrogen (N) or through one of its oxygens (O), which will be specified as nitrito- $$\mathrm{N}$$ or nitrito- $$\mathrm{O}$$, respectively.
  2. The prefixes di-, tri-, etc., are used to indicate the number of each type of ligand in the complex, but these prefixes do not affect the alphabetical order.
  3. The metal's oxidation state is indicated in parentheses using Roman numerals after the name of the metal.
  4. If the complex is an anion, the suffix "-ate" is added to the name of the metal. However, since cobalt is not in anionic form here, we don’t add "-ate" to cobalt.
  5. The charge of the complex can be determined for naming purposes, but it does not directly affect the choice among these options since they all refer to cobalt with Roman numerals to represent its oxidation state.

In this complex, cobalt ($\mathrm{Co}$) is the central metal ion, and its oxidation state needs to be determined. Considering the ligands, we have two chloro ($\mathrm{Cl}^-$) ligands, one nitrito ligand that we need to establish is binding through nitrogen for certain options, and three ammine ($\mathrm{NH}_3$) ligands.

To determine the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Co}$, recall that $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is a neutral ligand, $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ has a -1 charge each, making a total of -2 from the chlorides, and assuming nitrito as a monodentate ligand, it does not impact the calculation of the oxidation state as it's also considered to have a -1 charge when bound. The complex is neutral, so:

$x + 3(0) + 2(-1) + (-1) = 0$

$x - 3 = 0$

$x = +3$

So, cobalt is in the +3 oxidation state, making our choice among those mentioning cobalt(III).

The correct IUPAC name, therefore, comes from ordering the ligands alphabetically (ammine before chloro and nitrito), indicating the ligand count (triammine for three $\mathrm{NH}_3$, dichloro for two $\mathrm{Cl}^-$), and specifying how nitrito is bound if specified (nitrito-$\mathrm{N}$ or nitrito-$\mathrm{O}$). Since none of the names mention the nitrito binding as $\mathrm{O}$-bound, and we don't have evidence from the provided formula to assume it binds through oxygen, the choice defaults to either directly listing nitrito or specifying as nitrito-$\mathrm{N}$ if such specification is given.

Considering all of the above, the correct option is:

Option A: Triamminedichloridonitrito- $$\mathrm{N}-\operatorname{cobalt}(\mathrm{III})$$ because it follows the correct naming order, indicates the oxidation state accurately, and specifies the ligand connections correctly for a neutral complex.

content:
Match the Coordination compounds given in Column I with their characteristic features listed in Column II.

S.No. Coordination compounds S.No. Characteristic features
W $$
\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3
$$
 P $$
\text { Oxidation state }=+3 \text { Configuration }=\mathrm{d}^5 \quad \mu=5.92 \mathrm{BM}
$$
X $$
\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right.
$$		 Q $$
\text { Oxidation state }=+3 \text { Configuration }=\mathrm{d}^3 \quad \mu=3.87 \mathrm{BM}
$$
Y $$
\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_3(\mathrm{py})_3\right]
$$		 R $$
\text { Oxidation state }=+3 \text { Configuration }=d^6 \quad \mu=0 \mathrm{BM}
$$
Z $$
\mathrm{Cs}\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]
$$		 S $$
\text { Oxidation state }=+2 \text { Configuration }=\mathrm{d}^5 \quad \mu=1.732 \mathrm{BM}
$$
explanation:
To determine the correct matches between the coordination compounds in Column I and their characteristic features in Column II, let's analyze each compound's central metal ion, their oxidation states and electron configurations, and then connect them with the appropriate characteristic features:

Compound W: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{NH}_3)_5\mathrm{Cl}\right]\mathrm{Cl}_2$$

- Central metal: Cobalt (Co)

  • Co is in the complex ion, so bonding Co(III) due to two outside $\mathrm{Cl}^-$, must bring the oxidation state to +3. Electron configuration with Co(III) in a low spin complex (due to $\mathrm{NH}_3$) is typically $\mathrm{d}^6$ with a magnetic moment close of 0 since it is diamagnetic or very low.

Compound X: $$\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$$

- Central metal: Manganese (Mn)

  • Assuming Mn is the central ion and the charge of $\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}$ with each $\mathrm{CN}^-$ contributing -1. The oxidation state of Mn is +2. Manganese in this state $(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+})$ will have a $\mathrm{d}^5$ configuration. Because $\mathrm{CN}^-$ ion is a strong field ligand, the complex might be low spin, but $\mathrm{d}^5$ will still have a higher magnetic moment.

Compound Y: $$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_3(\mathrm{py})_3\right]$$

- Central metal: Chromium (Cr)

  • With a neutral ligand and typical counting, $\mathrm{Cr}$ is in the +3 oxidation state, giving an electron configuration of $\mathrm{d}^3$. This typical configuration and being octahedral could have a greater magnetic moment due to having 3 unpaired electrons.

Compound Z: $$\mathrm{Cs}\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]$$

- Central metal: Iron (Fe)

  • The complex ion $\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_4\right]^-$ suggests Fe is in the +3 oxidation state $(\mathrm{Fe}^{3+})$, and as such, iron would have a $\mathrm{d}^5$ electron configuration, which in a tetrahedral geometry usually retains more unpaired electrons, hence $\mu=5.92 \mathrm{BM}$ as more typical.

Now, by matching the above analysis with the characteristic features given:

- W should match with R $(\mathrm{d}^6, \mu=0)$,

  • X should match with S $(\mathrm{d}^5, \mu=5.92 \mathrm{BM})$,

  • Y should match with Q $(\mathrm{d}^3, \mu=3.87 \mathrm{BM})$,

  • Z should match with P $(\mathrm{d}^5, \mu=5.92 \mathrm{BM})$.

Option D correctly matches the compounds:

$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{R}, \quad \mathrm{X}=\mathrm{S}, \quad \mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{Q}, \quad \mathrm{Z}=\mathrm{P}$

content: Which one of the following is an incorrect statement pertaining to the properties of Coordination compounds?

options.content: A square planar complex of the type Mabcd, where a, b, c and d are unidentate ligands, exhibits geometrical isomerism and exists in one cis-form and two trans-forms.
[$$\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}_2$$] Cl<sub>2</sub> exists in 2 forms, the red form and the yellow form which are linkage isomers.
$$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$$ is called a Low spin complex.
Out of cis- $$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_2(\mathrm{ox})_2\right]^{3-}$$ and trans- $$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_2(\mathrm{ox})_2\right]^{3-}$$, the trans isomer is optically inactive.

explanation:
The incorrect statement among the given options is Option A.

Explanation:

Option A: The statement claims that a square planar complex of type $$\mathrm{Mabcd}$$, where each of $$\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}$$ are unidentate ligands, can exhibit geometric isomerism including one cis-form and two trans-forms. However, this is incorrect. In a square planar complex with four different ligands, there cannot be two different trans-forms, because the term 'trans' describes ligands that are directly opposite each other. With four different ligands, there is only one arrangement that can be considered trans (with any two specific ligands opposite each other). Therefore, a square planar complex $$\mathrm{Mabcd}$$ can indeed have a cis-form but only one possible trans-form. This error makes Option A incorrect.

To clarify using positional notation, if we assign the ligands as follows in a square plane:

  • a at position 1

  • b at position 2

  • c at position 3

  • d at position 4

A trans-configuration, for instance, could be where 'a' is trans to 'c' and 'b' is trans to 'd'. Any other arrangement merely reshuffles these positions but doesn't offer a fundamentally different 'trans' scenario, where a direct geometric opposition of ligands changes.

Option B: This is correct. $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}_2\right]$$ $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$ does exist in two forms – one where $$\mathrm{NO}_2$$ acts as a ligand through nitrogen (nitro) and another through oxygen (nitrito). These forms are indeed linkage isomers, displaying different properties and colors.

Option C: The statement is correct. $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{CN}\right)_6\right]^{3-}$$ is a low spin complex. Cyanide is a strong field ligand causing the electrons in iron to pair up into the lower energy orbitals (d-orbitals), resulting in a low spin state.

Option D: This statement is accurate. cis- and trans- $$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_2(\mathrm{ox})_2\right]^{3-}$$ differ in the spatial arrangement of the chloride ligands. The trans-isomer, where the chloride ligands are placed opposite each other, will be optically inactive because it has a mirror plane dividing it into two symmetrical halves, thus showing no optical activity.

content: Which one of the following Coordination entities exhibits Facial and Meridional isomerism?
explanation:
The phenomenon of facial and meridional isomerism occurs in octahedral complexes with a general formula of the type $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$, where A and B are different sets of bidentate or monodentate ligands. This type of isomerism describes the distinct arrangement positions these ligands can adopt relative to each other in the geometry of the complex.

Facial (fac) isomerism occurs when the three identical ligands occupy adjacent positions at the corners of one face of the octahedron, forming a triangle. This can be thought of as the three A ligands or three B ligands clustering on the same face of the octahedral structure.

Meridional (mer) isomerism, on the other hand, is characterized by the arrangement where the three identical ligands lie along a meridian roughly forming a vertical band that includes the metal center. This isomer has the three A ligands or three B ligands spread out, each occupying a vertex of a plane that cuts through the center of the octahedron and divides it into two equal halves.

Examining the options provided:

Option A: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_3\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_3\right]$$ is an octahedral complex where we have three molecules of water and three nitro groups as ligands. This type of complex has the general formula $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$, and thus can exhibit both facial and meridional isomerism, making it a possible answer.

Option B: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text{en})_2\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$$ does not fit the required general formula $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$ because it contains two ethylenediamine (en) ligands and two chloride ions. This complex is also more suitable to show geometrical isomerism (cis/trans).

Option C: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Br}_2\right] \mathrm{NO}_3$$ does not fit $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$ formula either, as it has four ammonia and two bromide ligands, making it suitable for other types of isomerism like cis-trans but not facial or meridional.

Option D: $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text{en})_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3$$ does not fit the formulational criterion $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$; it is a complex with three identical chelate rings (triethylenediamine ligands), and symmetry precludes it from fac or mer isomerism.

Conclusion: Among the options, Option A $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_3\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_3\right]$$ is the only complex capable of exhibiting both facial and meridional isomerism due to its meeting the $$\text{MA}_3\text{B}_3$$ configuration criteria. The other options do not conform to this requirement and thus cannot exhibit these specific types of isomerism.

content: The complex which does not show optical isomerism is
explanation: Octahedral complex of general formula $${[M{(AA)_2}{a_2}]^{n \pm }}$$ shows optical isomerism. $$cis - {[Co{(N{H_3})_4}C{l_2}]^ + }$$ does not show optical isomerism due to symmetry, while other three complexes show optical isomerism.

content: The oxidation state of nickel in [Ni(CO$$_4$$)] is
explanation:
[Ni(CO$$_4$$)]

Therefore, oxidation state of Ni is zero.

content: Which type of ligand is EDTA?
explanation: EDTA is a hexadentate ligand because it has six binding sites i.e. it has six donor atoms per ligand.

content: The correct IUPAC name of the coordination compound K$$_3$$[Fe(CN)$$_5$$NO] is
explanation:
K$$_3$$[Fe(CN)$$_5$$NO]

(CN)$$_5$$ = pentacyano

(NO) = nitro-N

So, IUPAC name of given compound is potassium pentacyanonitro-N-ferrate (III).

content: IUPAC name of Na$$_3$$[Co(NO$$_2$$)$$_6$$] is
explanation: IUPAC name of Na$$_3$$[Co(NO$$_2$$)$$_6$$] is sodium hexanitro cobaltate (III)

content: The number of unidentate ligands in the complex ion is called
explanation:
The number of unidentate ligands in the complex ion is called coordination number. Coordination is the number of lone pair of electron shared by ligands with central metal atom.

If all the ligand are unidentate, then number of ligand is equal to coordination number.

content: The oxidation state of iron in K$$_4$$[Fe(CN)$$_6$$] is,
explanation:
Oxidation state of iron (x) in K$$_4$$[Fe(CN)$$_6$$] is

$$4+x+6\times(-1)=0$$

$$x=2$$

content: Which complex cannot ionise in solution?
explanation: The complex [CoCl$$_3$$(NH$$_3$$)$$_3$$] cannot ionise in the solution as there is no primary valency in this compound and all the ions are within the coordination entity.
